author: @RickCochet-x8i
text:
I loved his videos, his insights, his enthusiasm. 
I hadn't kept up lately, and I'm sorry that I couldn't spend more time with an old friend.

replies.author: @HeirloomTomato-w6k
replies.text: Indeed, rest in peace Robert. Even after life, he is still showing us cool stuff.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
I just realized something that could be quite useful is a mold you can print out for making timing belts, then you can use fiberglass or nylon fibers and a moldable rubber to squeeze into and over the fibers to make a timing belt of the size of the mold, now to be able to make a variable mold so you can adjust it LOL 

(been thinking of this whole belt thing as I have three "broken" motors that I want to put a speed controllable DC motor on a belt system to run them, one a core from a grinder to hold diamond coated metal plates for grinding carbide (tungsten) parts like drills and mills for my PCB work I am in the middle of (and how you can get 10 drill bits made of solid carbide including the whole shank that on snapping off the bit you have a decent long shank that a small PCB depth level drill bit can be then ground down of the nearly diamond tough material which I have sorta done clumbsily by hand and it works but not fast enough) 

oh sorry the price, I paid a USD 1 dollar for each pack of those 10 drill bits, that the bit itself is .0188" and the shank is .125" (1/8th inch) diameter, 

I got them to make chisels for carving die steel for making coin dies for a friend's project but basically I paid 19 dollars for 190 bits and 10 dollars shipping (had to buy a small amount to check them out else that would have been one 5 dollar payment for all 19 as the guy is really good about their business practices and not trying to gouge people on shipping to make not a lot of money but ALL the money like some corpo minded person, also he makes VERY good bits (got some he made from blanks of carbide and coated himself with zirconia as well as uncoated end mills and they are SMOOOTH running very well made :) *(look up the oliver tool company or the user name drillman1 on ebay to find him :) ) anyway he had to raise his prices and he sells those 10 packs as used and reground but they are not, at least the 19 packs I got are ALL brand new and will bite quick into anything even solid blocks of glass and drill through it (granite you name it, they would as is make very good bead making drills for any stone other than natural Silicon Carbide or diamond (not sure if Tungsten carbide is a natural rock or not) also they are all japanese and a micrograin carbide of some sort so they dont just snap with the slightest flex 

BE CAREFUL opening the packs I have a whole half inch long drill bit in under my thumb nail from slipping while opening it and jabbing and snapping it off under the nail that is taking forever to migrate out as I have not sliced the nearly quarter inch deep hole into my thumb to be able to get a pair of tweezers or something on it to pull it out, and I have used my fingernail to flick a chip of carbide out of the white of my eye before (since the magnet trick does not work on tungsten LOL) and had to allow a cat allergy to cause my eyes to make a ton of eye boogers to get a very fine sharp flake of aluminum off the cornea of my eye too (was too painful to try and remove it and it most likely would have broken up possibly migrating into the cornea from what I had read, lol) 

anyway a slower moving belt fed very tight flat with that 90º angle that angle grinders and bench grinders have on the plate is just what I need though I also have a decent I think 5000W high speed routing head (water cooled) that I bought cheap as it was burned out, so I have a good core and bearings with a perfectly newish ER20 collet that running it slower would be better for milling metal and such, that I also want to belt feed a DC reversable speed controllable motor too for running it,. (working on a CNC T-slot rig to make a grinder head and slap a rotary table I have on the bed to make these bits. probably going to run it slow enough that a spring action on the holder of the bit I am grinding, can move should the grinding head is not moving perfectly smooth so it wont snap those carbide parts that refuse to deform the way even HSS does. lol. (ie a free floating head on a light spring since pressure is only going to wear down the grinding grit) 

anyway I will probably just use strapping tape and timing belt on a roll to make what I need right now but a way to make molds for them, man this gives me ideas like step down rotary tables on a CNC rig with multiple gear ratios on one unit so it can be dialed down to 1/1000th of a degree per step of the steppers ect. :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I was thinking about timing belts and 3D printing them only recently - i may try and do a video on it because you posted this mate

author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
text:
Hello Robert.  Nice video, as usual.  You've just made a light go off in my head - I'm currently resurrecting a milling machine, and need a bevel gear set for the vertical head I'm making.  Now, I can make straight cut bevel gears on the machine itself (which is how the bevel gears on the vertical axis drive and in the temporary vertical head were made), but they are noisy, and should really be helical.  The light you've just made go foo was the one marked "why not check out the price to get a pair of gears printed in stainless" - getting them cut is absolutely out of the question financially, I'm afraid, none of my friends have a gleason gear cutter.

So, before I go off wasting a bunch of time searching for a helical bevel gear generator, or worse, writing my own, I don't suppose you have a link for anything like that stocked away?

replies.author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
replies.text:
For what it's worth, I found a solution.  A couple, in fact.  Firstly, the BOSL2 extensions for OpenSCAD do this extremely easily.  Well, fairly easily.  Well, fairly easily for an OpenSCAD solution.  Yeah, it's not overly easy, but the results are great.  Chucked the resultant stl to the various online metal 3d printing services, yowza, spendy.

Second solution, one of the online metal 3d printing services which is frequently mentioned as a sponsor for youtube videos has almost-compatible gears available off the shelf for much less spendy.  But shipping is - well, spendy.

So I guess I'll be cutting the gears myself and making do with straight cut.  Bah.

author: @dav1dbone
text: I used the same gear generator to make a ring gear for a Swytch ebike hub motor, had planned on using it as a pattern for casting in brass, amazingly it held up just fine out of plastic

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text:
I started working with Inkscape and using SVG several years ago because it is scalable without loss, as opposed to pixel based graphics. My plan was to use it to make certain types of art in a video game /  electrical system simulation in Unreal Engine. Inkscape is a fairly amazing free SVG editor. 
I had no idea that Inkscape could be used to export to DXF... That's pretty cool stuff. Always cool to learn knew things here and there. I don't really use DXF as much as I used to, but it is handy to have for the occasional 2D drawing, CNC etc. I tend to do everything in 3D models, and convert to STL.
Thanks for making this video.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: thanks for the tips on inkscape mate - i am aware of it but never used it - i will have to change that lol
